retrieved_docs.title: Refractive Index -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Determining the rate at which an electromagnetic wave travels through a non-vacuum substance or the phase velocity, can be difficult considering the multitude of electromagnetic interactions occurring between charged particles. As an incident wave of light enters a dilute gas, its electric field polarizes the electrical field of the gas so that a new sine motion of the electrons is generated. The resultant energy radiates a new field onto the initial electric field, which vibrates out of phase with the original field but at the same frequency. The difference in the vibration phase is the resultant phase velocity by which light travels, thus determining that substance's index of refraction. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Nuclear Medicine Physics -- Definition/Introduction -- Types of Decay
retrieved_docs.content: Isomeric transition: After the isobaric transition, excess energy is emitted in the form of isomeric transition, mostly by emitting gamma photons. The energy of these gamma photons is variable and depends on the intermediate and final states of the nucleus undergoing an isomeric transition. These packets of released energy in the form of gamma photons often provide a unique fingerprint for different radionuclides, as each releases a predictable amount of energy to reach its stable state. The intermediate states can be short, or as in the case of “metastable states”, can be long, as is the case with technetium 99-metastable radionuclides used in imaging. Technetium 99-metastable radionuclides release 140 keV photons before reaching their stable ground state.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Tertiary Protein Structure -- Fundamentals -- Tertiary Structure of Proteins
retrieved_docs.content: Unlike secondary structures, which involve only hydrogen bonds between backbone components, tertiary structures result from diverse bonds and interactions between R-groups or between R-groups and the backbone. The key chemical bonds involved include hydrogen bonding, which occurs between polar side chains and opposite partial charges either in the peptide backbone or other R-groups; hydrophobic interactions in an aqueous solution, where water molecules interact with the hydrophilic polar side chains of a polypeptide, forcing the hydrophobic nonpolar side chains to merge into globular masses; and van der Waals interactions. Once hydrophobic side chains are close, their association is further stabilized by electrical attractions known as van der Waals interactions. [6] [7] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Xenon -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Xenon is an element with the symbol Xe, has an atomic number 54, and belongs to group 18 of the periodic table. Xe is a monoatomic, inert gas, first discovered in 1898 by British chemists William Ramsay (1852-1916) and Morris Travers (1872-1961) in the residue left after partial evaporation of liquid air (with krypton as an impurity). [1] Xe is a colorless, odorless, non-pungent, nontoxic, nonexplosive, and environmentally friendly noble gas. Xe exists in trace amounts in the Earth's atmosphere, with a concentration of only 0.086 ppm, and is also found in gases emitted from certain mineral springs. The name Xe is derived from the Greek word for "stranger," which reflects its rarity. Of its 9 naturally occurring isotopes, Xe-132 is the most abundant.

retrieved_docs.title: Bacterial DNA Mutations -- Function -- Physical Mutagens
retrieved_docs.content: Examples of physical mutagens include radiation or UV exposure. UV radiation damages DNA by creating covalent linkages between adjacent pyrimidine bases. This pyrimidine dimer cannot fit well in the double helix structure of DNA, thus inhibiting replication and translation. However, dimer formation usually results in a deletion mutation. Other types of radiation can have a variety of effects (Depending on intensity and wavelength), but mostly insertions/deletions occur. Purine dimers rarely occur. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Gadolinium Magnetic Resonance Imaging -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Gadolinium-based contrast agents are paramagnetic; that is, these atoms act like ferromagnetic and superparamagnetic substances and have a positive magnetic susceptibility. The effect of paramagnetic substances is several orders of magnitude weaker than that of other substances with positive susceptibility. Paramagnetic atoms have independent magnetically diffused moments. The induced magnetization returns to zero when the applied magnetic field is turned off.

retrieved_docs.title: Mass Spectrometer -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Atoms and molecules must first be ionized before being accelerated through the mass spectrometer and detected. [13] The sample molecule introduced into the mass spectrometer first gets a positive charge from an ionization source. This positive charge is achieved by removing a valence electron. Alternatively, protons can also be added to create a positive electrical charge. Once ionized, the molecule breaks apart into smaller fragments, then separated according to their mass-to-charge ratio in the mass analyzer. [1] Of note, only the cationic fragments are separated. The neutral species in the mass spectrometer go undetected as they are either absorbed by the apparatus or removed by a vacuum. After the ions are separated, the detector quantifies the ions. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Nuclear Medicine Physics -- Definition/Introduction -- Types of Decay
retrieved_docs.content: Alpha decay: Alpha decay occurs when the nucleus emits an alpha particle. An alpha particle is essentially a helium-4 atom and consists of 2 protons and 2 neutrons. Alpha particles have a charge of +2 and are emitted by heavy nuclides.

retrieved_docs.title: Nuclear Medicine Physics -- Definition/Introduction -- Types of Decay
retrieved_docs.content: Electron capture: Electron capture results from the “capture” of a negatively charged electron by a proton-heavy nucleus, resulting in a neutron. This electron is usually located in the K shell of an atom. The process of electron capture is an isobaric transition, as the atomic mass (ie, the total number of protons and neutrons) remains unchanged. The atomic number, based on the number of protons in the nucleus, decreases since a proton is now transformed into a neutron. The electron capture process is also an isomeric transition, resulting in the emission of photons that can be utilized for imaging purposes.

retrieved_docs.title: Electrophoresis -- Introduction -- Factors Affecting the Electrophoretic Mobility of a Molecule
retrieved_docs.content: Particles with a negative charge (anions) always move in the direction of the positive pole, whereas particles with a positive charge (cations) always move in the direction of the negative pole. When performing gel electrophoresis, the positive pole refers to the anode, whereas the negative pole refers to the cathode. As a result, charged particles move to the nodes that are appropriate for them. In gel electrophoresis, anions migrate from the cathode (−) to the anode (+). [2] Strength of the electrical field: Mobility is proportional to the potential gradient (voltage) and inversely proportional to resistance.

retrieved_docs.title: Bacterial DNA Mutations -- Function -- Flat Aromatic Compounds
retrieved_docs.content: Acridines like ethidium bromide can intercalate with adjacent pyrimidine base pairs. This interaction slightly unwinds the helix and increases the distance between adjacent base pairs. This intercalation disrupts the reading frame during translation and can cause insertions or deletions. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Primidone -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: The mechanism of action of primidone is not well-known, but it appears to bind centrally with voltage-gated sodium channels and inhibits the monotonous firing of action potentials. [9] Primidone also activates gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA)-A receptor complex with chloride ionophore, which extends the frequency of opening of the chloride channel, causing hyperpolarization by altering the electrical activity of the nerve cell membrane. [10] The effect of primidone in treating essential tremor is not mediated by the active metabolite PEMA. [11] Because in vivo investigations proved that primidone inhibits TRPM3 and attenuates thermal nociception, this drug could have a role in pain medicine. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Mass Spectrometer -- Testing Procedures -- Ionization
retrieved_docs.content: The most common form of ionization used in gas chromatography/mass spectrometry (GC/MS) is electron ionization. This method requires a source of electrons in the form of a filament to which an electric potential is applied, typically at 70 eV. [24] The molecules in the source are bombarded with high-energy electrons, resulting in the formation of charged molecular ions and fragments. Molecules break down into characteristic fragments according to their molecular structure. [25] The resulting ions and their relative proportions are reproducible, enabling the qualitative identification of compounds. [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Electric Potential and Capacitance -- Definition/Introduction -- The Concept of Charge
retrieved_docs.content: Electric potential and capacitance originate from the concept of charge. The charge is determined by comparing the number of protons and electrons present in a material. A net positive charge indicates more protons than electrons. Conversely, a net negative charge denotes more electrons than protons. An equal number of protons and electrons indicates a neutral charge. Materials with charge also exhibit electrical forces—opposite charges attract (for example, positive and negative) and similar charges repel (for example, positive and positive or negative and negative). The unit of measurement for the charge is a coulomb (C). Protons and electrons have a charge of +1 or +1.602×10 −19 coulombs and −1 or −1.602×10 −19 coulombs, respectively. These charge values are considered the elementary charge because the accumulation of microscopic electrons and protons determines the macroscopic charge of the material. This concept is pertinent when considering polymers comprised of several materials whose compositions are studied under an electric field. [1] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Nuclear Medicine Physics -- Definition/Introduction -- Types of Decay
retrieved_docs.content: Beta positive decay: Beta positive decay occurs when an atom has an excess of protons (and a low number of neutrons). In this process, a positively charged proton is converted into a neutral neutron. This process results in the emission of a positron and a neutrino, a particle that is electrically neutral and has essentially zero mass. A positron is akin to a positively charged version of an electron. Depending on its energy, the positron traverses a short distance until its energy decreases to 1.02 MeV, at which point it encounters an electron, resulting in an annihilation event. The result of an annihilation event is the emission of two photons, each measuring 511 keV in energy, emitted 180 degrees opposite in direction from each other. This emission of 2 photons is the basis of PET imaging.

retrieved_docs.title: Mass Spectrometer -- Results, Reporting, and Critical Findings
retrieved_docs.content: M+2 peak: This is another small peak similar to the m+1 peak. Certain elements, such as chlorine and oxygen, have multiple isotopes, which is why these elements in a given compound can result in an m+2 peak. [46]

retrieved_docs.content: action is mediated by electrolyte modulation. Its broader relevance is highlighted by its fundamental presence in natural structures such as perleť, illustrating the compound’s versatility across pharmacological and biological domains.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 1 -- Community 244
retrieved_docs.content: the diluent. Collectively, these relationships demonstrate how specific active molecules, their parent or precursor compounds, and formulation excipients are organized within pharmacological classes and therapeutic products, providing a comprehensive view of their chemical taxonomy, mechanisms of action, and clinical applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 1945
retrieved_docs.content: Phenyl salicylate is classified as a carboxylic acid and/or carboxylic acid derivative‑containing product, indicating that it belongs to the broader chemical class of substances that incorporate a carboxyl functional group or its derivatives. This relationship underscores the chemical nature of phenyl salicylate and situates it within the larger family of carboxylic acid‑related compounds, which is relevant for understanding its pharmacological properties, formulation considerations, and potential therapeutic applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2378
retrieved_docs.content: Pyridine and/or pyridine derivative, guaietolin, and the generic category Ring Compound are all classified as heterocyklické sloučeniny (heterocyclic compounds). Each of these entities possesses a cyclic molecular structure that includes one or more heteroatoms such as nitrogen, oxygen, or sulfur within the ring. Recognizing that pyridine derivatives, guaietolin, and other ring compounds belong to the broader class of heterocyclic compounds is important for understanding their chemical behavior, potential pharmacological activity, and role in drug development, as heterocyclic scaffolds frequently contribute to the biological properties of therapeutic agents.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1616
retrieved_docs.content: Guaietolin and ring compounds are both classified as heterocyclic compounds, a category of organic molecules that contain ring structures incorporating heteroatoms such as nitrogen, oxygen, or sulfur. Recognizing guaietolin as a heterocyclic compound highlights its specific chemical behavior and potential pharmacological properties, while the broader class of ring compounds includes many heterocyclic structures that are important in medicinal chemistry. Understanding that both guaietolin and generic ring compounds belong to the heterocyclic class aids clinicians and researchers in predicting reactivity, planning synthesis pathways, and exploring therapeutic applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 2374
retrieved_docs.content: Lafutidine is chemically associated with the heterocyclic structures pyridiny and piperidiny, indicating that it can be mapped from these moieties. In turn, pyridiny is mapped to zinc pyrithione, suggesting a relationship between the pyridiny scaffold and the zinc‑pyrithione compound. These mappings illustrate the structural connections among lafutidine, pyridiny, piperidiny, and zinc pyrithione, which may be relevant for understanding their pharmacological or formulation properties.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1409
retrieved_docs.content: Calcium citrate is a chemical compound that is derived from citric acid, with citric acid serving as its parent molecule.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1373
retrieved_docs.content: Alkoholy is a broad chemical category that encompasses both chlorbutanol and heptaminol, indicating that these two compounds are classified as types of alkoholy. Recognizing chlorbutanol and heptaminol as members of the alkoholy class helps clarify their shared chemical characteristics and informs their pharmacological profiling and potential therapeutic applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1314
retrieved_docs.content: toxins, are employed to safely stimulate protective immune responses, and xenon, an inert gas with anesthetic properties, influences neuronal activity and cerebral physiology. Collectively, these diverse entities serve as important qualifiers for describing and investigating physiological mechanisms, disease processes, and therapeutic interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1896
retrieved_docs.content: The category “Elements, Ions and Simple Compounds” encompasses the chemical entities bor and fosfor as its subordinate members, indicating that both bor and fosfor are considered child components within this classification of elements, ions, and simple compounds.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1898
retrieved_docs.content: Fluorocholine is classified as a fluorine compound, indicating that its chemical structure contains fluorine atoms and it belongs to the broader group of sloučeniny fluoru.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 1368
retrieved_docs.content: The category “Elements, Ions and Simple Compounds” encompasses a hierarchical structure in which “bor” is listed as a child entity, indicating that “bor” is considered a subordinate component within this broader classification of chemical entities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1562
retrieved_docs.content: Ibopamine is classified as a substance with phenol structure, indicating that it belongs to the broader category of phenolic compounds characterized by a hydroxyl group attached to an aromatic ring. Recognizing ibopamine as a phenolic substance informs its pharmacological properties and potential interactions within medical contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1276
retrieved_docs.content: Elements, Ions and Simple Compounds is a broad classification that encompasses individual chemical elements such as bor and molybden as its child components. Both bor and molybden are elements that can exist as ions and participate in the formation of simple compounds, illustrating how this category groups together fundamental chemical species that share similar properties and behaviors in inorganic chemistry.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 888
retrieved_docs.content: Organic nitrogen compounds are a class of chemical entities that share a structural relationship with pyridine or pyridine derivatives, indicating that pyridine serves as a parent scaffold for many members of this group. Specific drugs that are classified as organic nitrogen compounds include pentazocin, the antidepressant mianserin, and its salt form mianserin hydrochloride, each of which inherits the pyridine‑based core. Additionally, dihydralazin is recognized as an organic nitrogen compound, reinforcing the broader inclusion of diverse pharmacologically active agents within this chemical class. The common pyridine ancestry of these compounds underlies shared physicochemical characteristics and contributes to their therapeutic actions, highlighting the importance of the pyridine scaffold in the design and classification of organic nitrogen–containing medications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 1498
retrieved_docs.content: Cilostazol is classified within the broader chemical categories of 1H‑tetrazole, amidy, and azoly, indicating that the drug possesses structural features characteristic of a 1H‑tetrazole ring, amide functionalities, and azole moieties. Recognizing that cilostazol belongs to these chemical families helps elucidate its pharmacological properties, potential drug interactions, and the rationale for its therapeutic effects.
